summary: I became deeply involved with the Rainbow Coalition around 1987 and 1988, particularly in Washington State. The coalition was a diverse group representing people of all colors, LGBTQI, and those with limited access, and it left a lasting impression on me about political inclusivity. I served as secretary under Larry Gossett's chairmanship, traveling across the state to places like Yakima, Spokane, and Bellingham to organize meetings and support community causes. We worked on important campaigns, including supporting Jesse Jackson and influencing state legislature politics, and I felt a strong sense of solidarity with the Rainbow as my political tribe.

I was born and raised in Chicago, Illinois, where my father was a precinct captain deeply involved in local politics. Growing up, I witnessed firsthand how he helped get people out to vote, especially elderly and those with limited access, which shaped my early understanding of community organizing. Chicago was where my political roots began, surrounded by a family committed to civic engagement. This foundation influenced my later involvement in national campaigns and the Rainbow Coalition.

As a teenager, I moved to California where I completed high school and college, and even got married there. While in California, I was asked to organize for Ted Kennedy's campaign but chose to stay to finish my degree instead. California was an important chapter in my life, both personally and politically, as it was a place where I balanced education and early political involvement.

After California, I moved to Minneapolis with my then-husband, who was active as a precinct officer in local politics. I often accompanied him to legislative district meetings and sometimes got involved in projects, which expanded my political experience. Minneapolis was a stepping stone in my journey before moving to Seattle and deepening my political activism.

Seattle became my home after Minneapolis, where I continued my political engagement and eventually became involved with the Washington State Rainbow Coalition. Although state meetings were held only once or twice a year in Seattle, I traveled extensively across Washington to support the coalition's work. Seattle was the base from which I connected with activists statewide and helped organize political efforts.

I was actively involved in Ted Kennedy's campaign during the 1980s and served as a national delegate for him. Organizing for Kennedy in districts like the 11th and 32nd, I even traveled to Arizona to help with his campaign efforts. Ted Kennedy personally called me after I was selected as a delegate, and we had meaningful conversations about campaign strategy, which made me feel valued and connected to national politics.

Jesse Jackson's 1988 campaign was a pivotal moment for me; I became a national delegate for him and got deeply involved with the Rainbow Coalition through his movement. The campaign registered seven million new voters and brought a diverse coalition of people to the political table. Working closely with Jackson's state campaign manager, Charles Rolland, I helped organize grassroots efforts and witnessed firsthand how his leadership inspired political inclusion and activism.

The 1988 Democratic National Convention was a significant event where the Jesse Jackson delegation made a strong impression nationally. It was inspiring to see how people of color and those with limited access could influence the political process on such a large stage. My experience as a delegate at this convention reinforced my belief in the power of inclusive politics and motivated me to continue working with the Rainbow Coalition.

Yakima was one of the key locations where the Washington State Rainbow Coalition held meetings in the late 1980s. I remember a powerful group photo from around 1988 or 1989 with about forty or fifty of us, which really moved me and made me feel connected to my political tribe. Traveling to Yakima and other cities was part of our effort to engage communities statewide and build solidarity.

During the Ted Kennedy campaign, I was asked to organize supporters in Arizona, which was a valuable experience for me. Although I was later invited to organize in California, I chose to stay there to complete my education. Arizona represented an early opportunity to expand my political organizing skills beyond my home districts.

Larry Gossett was the chair of the Washington State Rainbow Coalition when I joined, and I got to know him quite well through our work together. He asked me to serve as secretary of the coalition, a role I was elected to because of my ability to connect with people and my prior experience. Larry was a key leader who helped guide the coalition's statewide efforts and fostered a strong sense of community among us.

Spokane was one of the cities we traveled to frequently for Washington State Rainbow Coalition meetings. We often stayed overnight to attend these gatherings, which helped us connect with activists and supporters across the state. Spokane represented an important hub in our statewide political outreach during the late 1980s.

Bellingham was another community where the Rainbow Coalition held meetings and engaged with local activists. Traveling to Bellingham and other towns allowed us to hear directly from diverse communities and build a broad coalition. These trips were essential for maintaining the Rainbow's presence and influence throughout Washington State.

Centralia was among the various locations where the Rainbow Coalition convened for state meetings. Visiting places like Centralia helped us connect with grassroots activists and understand local concerns, strengthening our statewide network. These meetings were crucial for advancing the coalition’s goals and fostering solidarity.

Charles Rolland was the state campaign manager for Jesse Jackson during 1987 and 1988, and I worked closely with him during that time. Our collaboration was key to organizing efforts that supported the Rainbow Coalition and the broader political movement. Charles was instrumental in helping us coordinate campaign activities and community outreach.

Jesse Wineberry was a political figure we supported through the Rainbow Coalition’s efforts, particularly in helping him become Minority Whip. This was significant because it marked the first time an outside organization like ours influenced inside legislature politics in Washington State. Supporting Jesse Wineberry was part of our strategy to bring more diverse voices into political leadership.

The Rainbow Coalition maintained a dialogue with the Green Party by inviting their candidates to speak and learn about Green politics. We wanted to understand their perspectives and build bridges where possible, reflecting our commitment to inclusivity. This engagement was part of our broader effort to connect with progressive groups, unlike our stance toward the Republican Party.

We never really reached out to the Republican Party because we felt they were not inclusive and did not align with our values. Especially given their support for Donald Trump, the Republican Party was not a group we wanted to engage with or spend energy on. Our focus was on building a coalition that welcomed diversity and progressive change, which we did not see reflected in the Republicans.

Donald Trump's support by the Republican Party was a factor in why we chose not to engage with them politically. His politics and the party's alignment with him did not represent the inclusive and diverse coalition we were building with the Rainbow. For me, Trump symbolized the kind of leadership and policies that were contrary to our goals.

Mary Alice Theiler was the attorney for the Washington State Rainbow Coalition, and she played an important role in our organization. She often participated in our executive meetings, providing legal guidance and support. Having her expertise was invaluable as we navigated political and organizational challenges.
